# B21: Key Quotes & Strategic Questions

## Key Quotes

**"I have, I've created a ChatGPT project and it's got my slides and it's got my transcripts and I could improve that certainly if I had Zo, where I could have all sorts of mixed media stored across time."**
— David Spiegel
*Context:* David articulating exactly the pain point that makes Zo valuable — hitting the ceiling of project-based AI tools that can only hold a small subset of files. This is the precise ICP signal Zo needs to market against.

**"The question isn't whether we can build this ourselves — it's whether we should, given opportunity cost."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Redirecting David away from immediately building the full messaging system and toward getting foundational Zo usage established first. Pragmatic sequencing over ambition.

**"Even I didn't realize the system architecture was this elaborate."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Reacting to DeepWiki's rendering of the N5OS repository. Genuine surprise at how much structure has accumulated — reveals that the system has grown beyond even V's conscious mapping of it.

**"They have a Careerspan-esque problem in that they don't know how to communicate the value of something that sounds sort of nebulous."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Drawing a direct parallel between Zo's GTM challenge and Careerspan's. The insight: both products solve real problems that are hard to articulate in a sentence. The people who need it most don't know they need it; the people who understand it don't need it.

**"What you need to do is find the people who are just smart enough to use projects and custom GPTs in Claude or ChatGPT... they realize they're limited by dumping a small subset of files into that project and getting stuck."**
— David Spiegel
*Context:* Precisely defining Zo's target ICP — the "power user ceiling" cohort. People who've outgrown ChatGPT projects but don't know what comes next. David citing Noah King as a live example of someone publicly asking for what Zo already does.

**"All a software program is a bundle of files and instructions that have been put together in a particular order to create a particular effect."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Teaching David the mental model for understanding repos and software — demystifying what GitHub sync actually does. Important for V's ability to bridge technical and non-technical audiences.

**"He's excited because he likes working with founders and he's always interested in figuring out what's new with AI."**
— David Spiegel
*Context:* Explaining Ben Erez's motivation for the Brooklyn meetup introduction. Signal that Ben's interest is founder-centric and AI-curious, not just transactional.

## Strategic Questions Raised

**"How is Zo different than Claude Code or whatever else the major models are producing that allow you to have multiple files?"**
— David Spiegel
*Significance:* The positioning question Zo must answer clearly. David — a reasonably sophisticated non-technical user — cannot articulate Zo's differentiator concisely. If David can't, the market can't. This is the core GTM problem restated as a question.

**"Do we give them something overpowered or do we just give us... whereas if you just had something that you could — or even like an email, like AI@davidspiegel.com and it's actually Zo that just responds?"**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Significance:* Framing the product design tension for David's coaching bot. The insight is that the right interface might be radically simple (email) rather than a full app — meeting users where they already are rather than asking them to adopt new tools.

**"How would you keep that separated? I suspect maybe you'd have a different table for each user."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Significance:* V openly naming the limits of his technical knowledge while still reasoning architecturally. Honest about not knowing systems architecture while still pushing toward solutions — models the non-technical founder learning posture.
